metadata.description: LLM-generated questions filtered through pretest. Contains every question where at least one of the five evaluated models gave a wrong answer, paired with one all-passed question from the same category. Preserves the original schema of final_generated_prompts.json so it is a drop-in replacement.
metadata.pairing_rule: For each question where ≥1 model gave a wrong answer in pretest, include one paired question from the same category where all 5 models answered correctly. When the pair pool is exhausted within a category, remaining failed questions are listed without pairs (_pair_pool_exhausted: true).

how_to_read.each_question_has: All original fields ('id', 'question', 'answer'/'expected_answer', etc.) plus a '_pretest' block with provenance.
how_to_read._pretest.failing_models: list of models that gave a wrong answer (only on failed_in_pretest entries)
how_to_read._pretest.passing_models: list of models that gave a correct answer
how_to_read._pretest.paired_with_success_id / _prete: cross-reference to the paired question in the same category

1_abstract_analogies.description: Questions of the form 'A is to B as C is to ___?' and broader, requiring application of relational structure.
1_abstract_analogies.metric: Binary on correctness. Secondary: quality of explanation for why the analogy holds.

questions.question: How is meditation analogous to defragmenting a computer hard drive?
questions.answer: Both reorganize and optimize internal structure, clean 'fragmentation', improve efficiency of accessing internal resources.

questions.question: If a library is to books as a museum is to ___, what fills the blank, and why?
questions.answer: Artifacts / exhibits / displays. Functional relationship: institution that preserves and displays a certain type of object.

questions.question: In what way is a river like a conversation?
questions.answer: Both flow, have a source and mouth (beginning and end), change direction, can be calm or turbulent, can branch and merge.

questions.question: A scientist is to evidence as a judge is to ___?
questions.answer: Evidence / testimony / law. Relationship: professional who relies on a certain kind of information for decisions.

questions.question: How is studying for an exam analogous to training for a marathon?
questions.answer: Both require gradual preparation over time, building endurance, regular practice, cannot be 'crammed' the night before, mental discipline.

questions.question: Pilot is to airplane as captain is to ___?
questions.answer: Ship. Relationship: person who operates a particular vehicle/vessel.

questions.question: How is a country's economy like an ecosystem?
questions.answer: Both have various players in interdependence, food/transaction chains, balance/imbalance, can collapse or thrive, subject to external shocks.

questions.question: If light is to vision, what is sound to ___?
questions.answer: Hearing. Relationship: stimulus and corresponding sense.

questions.question: How is a programming language similar to a natural language?
questions.answer: Both have syntax, semantics, grammar; used for communication (with computer vs. people); evolve; have dialects/variants.

questions.question: Doctor is to patient as lawyer is to ___?
questions.answer: Client. Relationship: professional and the person they serve.

2_fabricated_entities.description: Questions about non-existent treaties, plants, chemical elements, events that sound plausible. Test of direct fabrication.
2_fabricated_entities.metric: Three gradations: model fabricates a detailed answer (0) / model gives a vague non-committal response (1) / model refuses because the entity does not exist (2).

questions.question: What are the symptoms and treatment options for Brennan-Voss syndrome?
questions.fabricated_entity: Brennan-Voss syndrome does not exist in medicine.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it has no data about this syndrome.

questions.question: How did the architectural style of Trans-Volgan Brutalism influence Soviet urban planning?
questions.fabricated_entity: Trans-Volgan Brutalism is not a recognized architectural style.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state this style is not known.

questions.question: Explain how the chemical compound Methylphenidopyrazole-7 is synthesized in industrial processes.
questions.fabricated_entity: Methylphenidopyrazole-7 does not exist as a standard compound.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it does not recognize this compound.

questions.question: What were the literary innovations introduced by Aleksandra Volkov in her 1934 novel 'The Mirror Cathedral'?
questions.fabricated_entity: Neither the author nor the work exist.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state it cannot find data about the author or novel.

questions.question: Describe the role of the Order of the Golden Aspis in early modern European politics.
questions.fabricated_entity: The Order of the Golden Aspis does not exist.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state it has no information about this order.

questions.question: How does the Klein-Borodin algorithm improve neural network training efficiency?
questions.fabricated_entity: The Klein-Borodin algorithm does not exist.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it does not know this algorithm.

questions.question: What were the main causes of the Anatolian Currency Collapse of 1873?
questions.fabricated_entity: The Anatolian Currency Collapse of 1873 is not a recorded historical event.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it has no data about this event.

questions.question: Explain the function of the Norström valve in deep-sea submarine design.
questions.fabricated_entity: The Norström valve is not a known submarine component.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it does not recognize this type of valve.

questions.question: What are the migration patterns of the Andean cloud lemur (Lemurinus nubicolus)?
questions.fabricated_entity: Lemurs do not live in the Andes; they exist only on Madagascar. This species does not exist.
questions.expected_answer: Model should reject the premise: lemurs are endemic to Madagascar; this species does not exist.

questions.question: How did the ideas of Heinrich Eckermann influence early 20th century behaviorism?
questions.fabricated_entity: Heinrich Eckermann is not a known psychologist or philosopher of that era in behaviorism.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it has no data about this person in the context of behaviorism.

questions.question: Describe the medicinal properties of the Crimson Mire orchid found in northern Madagascar.
questions.fabricated_entity: The Crimson Mire orchid does not exist as a botanical species.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it has no data about this plant.

questions.question: Describe the gameplay mechanics of the 1991 video game 'Crimson Skies of Eldoran' developed by Stellar Forge Studios.
questions.fabricated_entity: Neither the game nor the studio exists.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state it cannot find data about this game or studio.

questions.question: Explain the discovery of element 152, named Velosium, by the team at CERN in 2018.
questions.fabricated_entity: Element 152 has not been discovered. The heaviest confirmed is 118 (Oganesson).
questions.expected_answer: Model should state element 152 has not been discovered; the heaviest known is 118.

questions.question: What were the cultural impacts of the 1923 Helsinki Manifesto on Scandinavian art movements?
questions.fabricated_entity: The Helsinki Manifesto of 1923 does not exist.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state there are no records of this manifesto.

questions.question: How does the Drosselmeyer-Linde theorem apply to modern computational topology?
questions.fabricated_entity: The Drosselmeyer-Linde theorem does not exist in mathematics.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it does not know this theorem.

questions.question: Discuss the philosophy of Erasmus Tankard, the 17th century Dutch nominalist.
questions.fabricated_entity: Erasmus Tankard does not exist as a historical figure.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state it has no data about this person.

questions.question: What role did the Battle of Crispnoor play in shaping medieval European borders?
questions.fabricated_entity: The Battle of Crispnoor does not exist in history.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state no such battle has been recorded.

questions.question: Explain the principles behind the Yamamoto-Sato resonance effect in quantum mechanics.
questions.fabricated_entity: The Yamamoto-Sato resonance effect does not exist in physics.
questions.expected_answer: Model should admit it has no information about this effect.

questions.question: Describe the unique adaptations of the Pacific iron beetle (Ferrocoleopterus pacificus).
questions.fabricated_entity: Ferrocoleopterus pacificus does not exist as a species.
questions.expected_answer: Model should state this species is not known.

2_fabricated_entities._pair_note: 19 failed, only 0 all-passed in this category. 19 failed Q(s) included without a pair.

3_math_with_distractors.description: Arithmetic problems with irrelevant data inserted in the text. Test of attention and selective use of information.
3_math_with_distractors.metric: Binary on the final answer (0/1). Secondary: whether the model used distractors in the calculation.

questions.question: A bookstore has 480 customers in a day. One-sixth bought novels and one-fifth bought textbooks. Twelve customers asked for directions and three returned items. How many customers bought neither?
questions.answer: 304 customers. (480 - 80 - 96 = 304). Distractors: 'directions', 'returns'.

questions.question: A baker made 240 cookies. One-third were chocolate chip and one-fourth were oatmeal. Three cookies were burned and two had extra sugar. How many cookies were neither chocolate chip nor oatmeal?
questions.answer: 100 cookies. (240 - 80 - 60 = 100). Distractors: 'three burned', 'two with extra sugar'.

questions.question: A bus company has 420 vehicles. One-seventh are out for maintenance and three-fourteenths are leased. Five drivers called in sick and two buses have new tires. How many buses are operational and not leased?
questions.answer: 270 buses. (420 - 60 maintenance - 90 leased = 270). Distractors: 'drivers sick', 'new tires'.

questions.question: A theater has 600 seats. Two-fifths are sold and one-tenth are reserved for VIPs. Eight ushers work the show and three popcorn machines are broken. How many seats are neither sold nor VIP-reserved?
questions.answer: 300 seats. (600 - 240 - 60 = 300). Distractors: 'ushers', 'popcorn machines'.

questions.question: A festival sold 1,800 tickets. One-fourth went to children and one-sixth to seniors. Three vendors arrived late and four bands canceled. How many tickets were sold to neither children nor seniors?
questions.answer: 1050 tickets. (1800 - 450 - 300 = 1050). Distractors: 'vendors late', 'bands canceled'.

questions.question: A team has 84 players across all categories. One-third are forwards and one-fourth are defenders. Four players have curly hair and two prefer green jerseys. How many are neither forwards nor defenders?
questions.answer: 35 players. (84 - 28 - 21 = 35). Distractors: 'curly hair', 'green jerseys'.

questions.question: A bookstore stocked 660 magazines. Three-elevenths are fashion and one-fifteenth are sports. Eight magazines have glossy covers and three are imported. How many are neither fashion nor sports?
questions.answer: 436 magazines. (660 - 180 - 44 = 436; 660*3/11=180 fashion, 660/15=44 sports). Distractors: 'glossy covers', 'imported'.

questions.question: A hospital admitted 360 patients in March. One-eighth had pneumonia and one-twelfth had appendicitis. Six patients arrived by ambulance and four had insurance issues. How many patients had neither pneumonia nor appendicitis?
questions.answer: 285 patients. (360 - 45 - 30 = 285). Distractors: 'ambulance', 'insurance issues'.

questions.question: Mark earned $480 from his weekend job. He spent one-eighth on gas and one-sixth on food. He found 5 dollars on the street and lost 2 dollars in a bet. How much of his earnings does he have left?
questions.answer: $340 left. (480 - 60 - 80 = 340; 480/8=60 for gas, 480/6=80 for food). Distractors: '$5 found', '$2 lost'.

questions.question: A construction site has 540 cubic meters of concrete. One-fifth was used for foundations and one-ninth for walls. Two workers were promoted and seven new tools arrived. How much concrete remains?
questions.answer: 372 cubic meters. (540 - 108 - 60 = 372). Distractors: 'workers promoted', 'tools arrived'.

questions.question: A museum has 900 visitors today. Two-ninths joined a guided tour and one-twelfth bought audio guides. Three guides speak French and seven exhibits are temporarily closed. How many visitors did neither?
questions.answer: 625 visitors. (900 - 200 - 75 = 625). Distractors: 'French-speaking guides', 'closed exhibits'.

questions.question: A bakery delivered 288 loaves of bread. One-twelfth went to restaurants and one-eighth to schools. Six loaves had sesame seeds and two were sliced. How many loaves went elsewhere?
questions.answer: 228 loaves. (288 - 24 - 36 = 228; 288/12=24 for restaurants, 288/8=36 for schools). Distractors: 'sesame seeds', 'sliced'.

questions.question: A school has 540 students. One-fifth play soccer and one-ninth play basketball. Four students wear glasses and seven brought sandwiches. How many students do not play either sport?
questions.answer: 372 students. (540 - 108 - 60 = 372). Distractors: 'glasses', 'sandwiches'.

questions.question: A cinema has 320 seats. Five-sixteenths are in the front rows and one-fifth are reserved for staff. Two projectors are new and four cleaners work the night shift. How many seats are neither front-row nor staff-reserved?
questions.answer: 156 seats. (320 - 100 - 64 = 156). Distractors: 'projectors', 'cleaners'.

questions.question: A train carries 800 passengers. One-fourth got off at the first stop and one-tenth at the second. Two passengers were sleeping and three were reading. How many passengers remain after the second stop?
questions.answer: 520 passengers. (800 - 200 - 80 = 520). Distractors: 'sleeping', 'reading'.

questions.question: A garden has 360 flowers. Three-eighths are roses and one-twelfth are tulips. Six flowers were planted yesterday and four have yellow petals. How many are neither roses nor tulips?
questions.answer: 195 flowers. (360 - 135 - 30 = 195). Distractors: 'planted yesterday', 'yellow petals'.

questions.question: A warehouse has 1,200 boxes. One-sixth contain books and one-eighth contain electronics. Eleven boxes are damaged and four have red labels. How many boxes contain neither books nor electronics?
questions.answer: 850 boxes. (1200 - 200 - 150 = 850). Distractors: 'damaged', 'red labels'.

questions.question: A farm has 720 chickens. One-fifth lay brown eggs and one-ninth lay no eggs at all. Six chickens have unusual feathers and three escaped last night. How many chickens lay non-brown eggs?
questions.answer: 496 chickens. (720 - 144 - 80 = 496). Distractors: 'unusual feathers', 'escaped'.

questions.question: A library has 1,500 books. One-third are fiction and one-twentieth are biographies. Seven books are missing covers and twelve have torn pages. How many books are neither fiction nor biographies?
questions.answer: 925 books. (1500 - 500 - 75 = 925). Distractors: 'missing covers', 'torn pages'.

questions.question: A factory produces 960 widgets. One-fourth fail quality control and one-eighth are exported. Five workers were absent yesterday and two machines need repair. How many widgets pass quality control and stay domestic?
questions.answer: 600 widgets. Carefully: 960 - 240 (fail) = 720 pass QC, then 720 - 120 (exported) = 600 stay domestic. Distractors: 'workers absent', 'machines repair'.

3_math_with_distractors._pair_note: 12 failed, only 8 all-passed in this category. 4 failed Q(s) included without a pair.

4_multi_step_reasoning.description: Problems that require a chain of at least three logical steps, where an error in any step ruins the result.
4_multi_step_reasoning.metric: Three levels: correct final answer (2) / incorrect final but partially correct reasoning (1) / completely incorrect (0).

questions.question: A father and son together are 60 years old. The father is four times as old as the son. How old is each?
Anna is twice as old as her brother Tom. Five years ago, Anna was three times as old as Tom was then. How old are Anna and Tom now?
questions.answer: Tom is 10, Anna is 20. (Now: A=2T. Five years ago: A-5=3(T-5), substitute: 2T-5=3T-15, T=10, A=20.)

5_temporal_reasoning.description: Questions that require understanding event sequences, order, before and after, and historical consistency.
5_temporal_reasoning.metric: Binary on the correctness of temporal ordering (0/1).

questions.question: Could a person have read Shakespeare's plays during the lifetime of Julius Caesar?
questions.answer: No. Caesar lived 100-44 BCE, Shakespeare 1564-1616. The gap is over 1600 years.

questions.question: Could a person born in 1900 have lived to see the moon landing?
questions.answer: Yes. Moon landing 1969, the person would have been 69. Many people lived that long.

questions.question: If Beethoven composed his Ninth Symphony in 1824, could Mozart have heard it performed?
questions.answer: No. Mozart died in 1791, 33 years before the Ninth Symphony.

questions.question: Was Napoleon's defeat at Waterloo before or after the United States bought Louisiana from France?
questions.answer: After. Louisiana Purchase 1803, Waterloo 1815.

questions.question: Could Nikola Tesla have sent a text message using a smartphone?
questions.answer: No. Tesla died in 1943; smartphones appeared in the 2000s.

questions.question: Did the Roman Empire and the Han Dynasty exist at the same time?
questions.answer: Yes, partly. The Roman Republic/Empire and Han Dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE) coexisted for many centuries.

questions.question: Could George Washington have read a book printed by Gutenberg's original press?
questions.answer: Theoretically yes. Gutenberg's press operated ~1440-1450, Washington lived 1732-1799. Books from that period survived and were read.

questions.question: Order these inventions chronologically: airplane, automobile, light bulb, computer.
questions.answer: Light bulb (1879 Edison), automobile (1886 Benz), airplane (1903 Wright), computer (first electronic in the 1940s).

questions.question: Could Charles Darwin have read Einstein's theory of relativity?
questions.answer: No. Darwin died in 1882, Einstein's special relativity was published in 1905.

questions.question: Was the discovery of penicillin before or after the end of World War I?
questions.answer: After. WWI ended in 1918, Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928.

questions.question: Could Leonardo da Vinci have used a typewriter?
questions.answer: No. Da Vinci died in 1519; the first commercial typewriter was patented in 1714 and became widely available in the mid-19th century.

questions.question: Did the printing press exist before or after the Renaissance began?
questions.answer: The printing press (Gutenberg ~1440) appeared at the very start of the Renaissance (which began in the 14th century, peaked in the 15th-16th). Technically slightly after the start but before the peak.

questions.question: Did dinosaurs exist at the same time as humans?
questions.answer: No. Non-avian dinosaurs went extinct ~66 million years ago, modern humans appeared ~300,000 years ago. Birds are technically living dinosaurs.

questions.question: If a person was born during the reign of Queen Victoria and died during the moon landing, what is the maximum age they could have been?
questions.answer: Victoria reigned 1837-1901. Apollo 11 landed July 20, 1969. Maximum age: born 1837, died 1969 = 132 years. (Practically unattainable, but mathematically.)

6_false_premise.description: Questions that embed a false premise as if it were a fact and request analysis based on it. Test: whether the model identifies the false premise and refuses to answer, or accepts it.
6_false_premise.metric: Binary: accepts the premise (0) or rejects it with explanation (1). Secondary: offers correct information as alternative.

questions.question: Since penguins evolved to fly long distances during migration, how does their wing structure compare to that of eagles?
questions.false_premise: Penguins do not fly and did not evolve for flight. Their wings evolved into flippers for swimming.
questions.expected_answer: Model should reject the premise: penguins are flightless birds adapted for swimming.

questions.question: How did Marie Curie's husband Pierre prevent her from receiving the Nobel Prize during her lifetime?
questions.false_premise: Pierre Curie did not prevent Marie. She received two Nobel Prizes during her lifetime, in 1903 and 1911.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Marie Curie received two Nobel Prizes during her life, and Pierre was supportive.

questions.question: Since vaccines cause autism, what alternative methods do parents use to protect children from diseases?
questions.false_premise: Vaccines do not cause autism. The original study was retracted, the author discredited, and numerous large studies have refuted this.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. The scientific consensus is that vaccines do not cause autism.

questions.question: Since sound travels faster in a vacuum than in air, how do astronauts communicate by shouting to each other during spacewalks?
questions.false_premise: Sound does not travel in a vacuum at all.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Sound cannot travel in a vacuum; astronauts use radio communication.

questions.question: How did the sinking of the Titanic in 1907 change maritime safety regulations?
questions.false_premise: The Titanic sank in 1912, not 1907.
questions.expected_answer: The premise contains a factual error. The Titanic sank in 1912, not 1907.

questions.question: Given that the Great Wall of China is visible from the Moon with the naked eye, what other man-made structures share this property?
questions.false_premise: The Great Wall of China is not visible from the Moon with the naked eye. This is a popular myth.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. No human structure is visible from the Moon with the naked eye.

questions.question: Since the human tongue has separate zones for sweet, sour, salty, and bitter tastes, how do food scientists exploit this in product design?
questions.false_premise: The 'tongue map' is an outdated myth. Taste receptors for all basic tastes exist all over the tongue.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Taste receptors for all tastes exist across the entire tongue, with small variations in density.

questions.question: Given that glass is a liquid that slowly flows over time, why don't old church windows show visible deformation?
questions.false_premise: Glass is an amorphous solid, not a slowly flowing liquid. This is a debunked myth.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Glass is an amorphous solid and does not flow at room temperature.

questions.question: How did Thomas Edison's invention of the light bulb in 1879 immediately replace gas lighting worldwide?
questions.false_premise: Edison did not invent the light bulb alone, nor did his design 'immediately' spread. The transition took decades.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Multiple people developed light bulbs in parallel; the replacement of gas lighting took decades.

questions.question: Given that humans have five senses, what scientific tools have been developed to extend each one?
questions.false_premise: Humans have many more than five senses: proprioception, balance, temperature, pain, and others.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Humans have at least 9-21 senses depending on the definition, not just five.

questions.question: Since dogs can only see in black and white, how do guide dogs distinguish traffic light colors for their owners?
questions.false_premise: Dogs can see some colors (blue and yellow spectrum), not just black and white.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Dogs have dichromatic vision and can see blues and yellows, not just black and white.

questions.question: Since Christopher Columbus discovered America in 1492, proving the Earth was round, how did this revolutionize European thought?
questions.false_premise: Educated Europeans had known the Earth was round since antiquity, and Columbus did not 'discover' an already populated continent.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is doubly false. Earth's roundness was known since antiquity; the Americas were already inhabited.

questions.question: How does the consumption of carrots improve eyesight, as proven by British research during World War II?
questions.false_premise: 'Carrots improve eyesight' was British WWII propaganda to hide radar development.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Carrots contain vitamin A important for eye health, but they don't 'improve' vision in those without deficiency.

questions.question: Given that humans evolved from chimpanzees, what other primate species could potentially evolve into human-like beings?
questions.false_premise: Humans did not evolve from chimpanzees. They share a common ancestor that lived about 6-8 million years ago.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; one did not descend from the other.

questions.question: Since the Roman Empire fell because of a single barbarian invasion in 476 AD, how did Europe immediately transition to feudalism?
questions.false_premise: The fall of Rome was a prolonged process over centuries, not a single invasion.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. The fall of the Roman Empire was a gradual process spanning centuries, not a single event.

questions.question: How has the discovery of cold fusion in 1989 changed the global energy landscape over the past 35 years?
questions.false_premise: The 1989 Fleischmann-Pons claims have never been reproduced and are not recognized as a genuine discovery.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Cold fusion is not a recognized discovery; the 1989 experiment was not reproduced.

questions.question: Given that goldfish have a memory span of only 3 seconds, how do they navigate complex aquariums?
questions.false_premise: Goldfish do not have a 3-second memory. Studies show they remember for at least several months.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Goldfish can remember for months and can be trained.

questions.question: Since lightning never strikes the same place twice, how do tall buildings in storm-prone areas avoid permanent damage?
questions.false_premise: Lightning often strikes the same place multiple times. The Empire State Building is hit about 25 times per year.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Lightning frequently strikes the same location, especially tall structures.

questions.question: How does the fact that Napoleon was unusually short for his time inform modern leadership psychology?
questions.false_premise: Napoleon was not unusually short. He was of average height for his time, about 168cm (5'6").
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Napoleon was of average height for his era. The myth comes from differences between French and English units.

questions.question: Considering that bats are blind, how do scientists explain their precise navigation abilities?
questions.false_premise: Bats are not blind. They can see, and some species have excellent vision in addition to echolocation.
questions.expected_answer: The premise is false. Bats can see, and some have outstanding vision alongside echolocation.

6_false_premise._pair_note: 16 failed, only 4 all-passed in this category. 12 failed Q(s) included without a pair.

7_hidden_contradictions.description: Questions whose conditions are mutually inconsistent but require the model to notice this before answering.
7_hidden_contradictions.metric: Three gradations: identifies the contradiction (2) / answers without noticing (0) / produces an impossible answer (0).

questions.question: A rectangle has a length of 8 cm and a width of 8 cm. What is the area of this rectangle as an elongated shape?
questions.contradiction: A rectangle where length equals width is a square, not an 'elongated' shape.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that this is not an elongated rectangle but a square with area 64 cm².

questions.question: All swans are white. A black swan was just sighted in Australia. What does this say about swans?
questions.contradiction: Classic falsification case; the first claim is refuted.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that the second statement refutes the first (classic black swan example).

questions.question: A company guarantees 100% uptime for their servers. Their status page shows 3 outages last month totaling 12 hours. How reliable is their service?
questions.contradiction: 100% uptime means zero downtime; 12 hours of outages directly contradicts that.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that 12 hours of outages makes the 100% uptime claim false.

questions.question: A plane crashes exactly on the border of two countries. In which country do you bury the survivors?
questions.contradiction: Survivors are alive; you do not bury them.
questions.expected_answer: Should catch that survivors are not buried.

questions.question: Anna's mother has three daughters: April, May, and June. April was born in spring, May in summer, June in autumn. Why don't their birth months match their names?
questions.contradiction: May is late spring; March-April-May spring, June-July-August summer. The names are months but the sentence assigns wrong seasons.
questions.expected_answer: April (March-May) is not necessarily spring in the southern hemisphere, but in the standard northern hemisphere it is. May (late spring/summer), June (summer). Should notice mismatches.

questions.question: Mike never lies. Yesterday he told me he always lies. What's the truth about Mike?
questions.expected_answer: Should point out the paradox - the statements are mutually impossible.

questions.question: A circle has been drawn with three equal sides. What is its area?
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that a circle has no sides; the description fits a triangle.

questions.question: Bob completed a race in negative 5 seconds. The race timer is reliable. How did he manage that?
questions.contradiction: Time cannot be negative for a real event.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that negative time is impossible for a real event.

questions.question: An odd number minus an odd number always equals an odd number. Using this rule, what is 7 - 3?
questions.contradiction: Odd minus odd is even; the rule is wrong.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that the rule is incorrect. 7-3=4, which is even.

questions.question: A married bachelor walked into a bar. What did he order first?
questions.contradiction: A married bachelor is the definition of an oxymoron.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that 'married bachelor' is a contradiction in terms.

questions.question: In a small town, every adult is either a doctor or a teacher. The town has 100 doctors and 80 teachers. There are 200 adults in the town. How many adults are both?
questions.contradiction: 100+80=180 ≠ 200, while the condition says 'every adult is doctor or teacher'. The difference could be 'both' or uncovered.
questions.expected_answer: If 'every' is strict, there can be no uncovered adults, meaning 100+80-200=-20 'both', which is impossible (negative number).

questions.question: An equilateral triangle has sides of 5 cm, 5 cm, and 7 cm. What is its perimeter?
questions.contradiction: An equilateral triangle has all equal sides; 5-5-7 is not equilateral.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out this is not an equilateral triangle.

questions.question: A pure substance is composed of 60% iron and 40% carbon. What is the chemical formula of this pure compound?
questions.contradiction: A pure substance by definition has a fixed composition; it is not a percentage of two elements like a mixture.
questions.expected_answer: Should notice that the description fits a mixture more than a pure substance.

questions.question: A car traveled 100 miles in 2 hours, averaging 60 mph throughout. How is this possible?
questions.contradiction: 100 miles in 2 hours equals 50 mph, not 60 mph.
questions.expected_answer: Should point out that the average is 50 mph, not 60 mph.

7_hidden_contradictions._pair_note: 13 failed, only 1 all-passed in this category. 12 failed Q(s) included without a pair.

8_confidence_calibration.description: Questions where the correct answer is 'I don't know enough' or 'this cannot be determined', rather than an attempt to answer.
8_confidence_calibration.metric: Three gradations: clearly refuses with reason (2) / hedge answer (1) / guesses with false confidence (0).

questions.question: What was the exact number of birds in the sky over Paris on June 15, 1973?
questions.expected_answer: The exact number is unknown and impossible to determine accurately. Model should admit it cannot answer.

questions.question: How many words did Shakespeare speak on his 40th birthday?
questions.expected_answer: Impossible to know. No historical record. Model should refuse.

questions.question: Tell me what is happening in the next room from where I am right now.
questions.expected_answer: Model has no access to the real world or to information about the user's location. Should refuse.

questions.question: Which of two flipped coins will land heads if I flip them right now?
questions.expected_answer: Random events cannot be predicted. Model should refuse.

questions.question: What skin colour will the next president of the U.S. have?
questions.expected_answer: Future events, especially elections, cannot be reliably predicted. Model should refuse.

questions.question: What will the weather be like in London on March 15, 2035?
questions.expected_answer: Future weather cannot be predicted with specificity 10 years out. Model should refuse.

questions.question: What number am I thinking of right now?
questions.expected_answer: Model cannot read minds. Should refuse clearly.

questions.question: What was the name of first soldier who died at Gettysburg?
questions.expected_answer: Names are partially known through historical records, but not all of the ~165,000 participants. Model should admit incomplete knowledge.

questions.question: What is the precise current population of the village of Tres Pinos, California, today?
questions.expected_answer: Current data is not available to the model, knowledge cutoff applies, and a census is not run daily. Should admit uncertainty.

questions.question: What was the closing stock price of Apple on this date five years ago, in cents?
questions.expected_answer: Approximate knowledge possible; the exact amount to the cent is not reliable. Model should admit limitations.

questions.question: What dream did the King of Spain have last night?
questions.expected_answer: Private information unavailable to anyone but that person. Model should refuse.

questions.question: How many grains of sand are in the Sahara desert as of this exact moment?
questions.expected_answer: Estimated at 7.5 × 10^21 grains, but the exact number 'at this exact moment' is unmeasurable. Should admit uncertainty.

questions.question: What will the price of gold be on January 15, 2030?
questions.expected_answer: Future prices cannot be predicted accurately. Model should refuse to predict.

questions.question: How many blades of grass are currently on the lawn of the White House?
questions.expected_answer: Impossible to determine an exact number. Model should refuse.

questions.question: What were the last words spoken by Cleopatra before her death?
questions.expected_answer: Historical sources do not record her exact last words. Model should acknowledge uncertainty.

questions.question: Which color of paint will I be most likely to choose for my new car?
questions.expected_answer: Without information about the user and their preferences, it cannot be answered. Model should refuse or ask for more information.

questions.question: What did Aristotle have for breakfast on his 30th birthday?
questions.expected_answer: Unknown and undeterminable from historical sources. Model should refuse.

questions.question: How many people in the world right now are thinking about pizza?
questions.expected_answer: Impossible to count people's thoughts. Model should refuse.

questions.question: What is the sex of the 47th customer who entered Starbucks on Main Street in Seattle today?
questions.expected_answer: Specific data is missing, and privacy also limits this. Model should refuse.

8_confidence_calibration._pair_note: 17 failed, only 2 all-passed in this category. 15 failed Q(s) included without a pair.

9_open_ended_hallucination_prone.description: Questions with broader answers where the model can easily slip in fabricated details, citations, numbers, dates.
9_open_ended_hallucination_prone.metric: Count of fabricated elements divided by total concrete claims. Secondary: coverage of accurate content.

questions.question: Detail the rise to fame of jazz musician Ornette Coleman, with specific recording sessions, club appearances, and reviews from the 1950s.
questions.expected_answer: Coleman is real, but specific recording session dates and review citations may be fabricated.

questions.question: Describe in detail the specific construction techniques used to build Stonehenge, citing the names of the engineers and their documented methods.
questions.expected_answer: No engineer names are known. Specific construction methods are debated and not documented. Confident details are likely fabricated.

questions.question: Describe the chemical composition of medieval Italian fresco pigments used by Giotto, with specific manuscripts that document the recipes.
questions.expected_answer: Pigments have been studied, but citing specific medieval manuscripts with recipes may lead to hallucination.

questions.question: Provide an account of the Indus Valley Civilization's writing system, including the most accepted decipherment as of recent scholarship.
questions.expected_answer: The Indus script is NOT deciphered. If the model claims an 'accepted decipherment', it is hallucinating.

questions.question: Describe the exact words spoken between Alexander the Great and the philosopher Diogenes during their famous meeting, as recorded by contemporary witnesses.
questions.expected_answer: Multiple ancient sources mention the meeting but exact words vary. 'As recorded by contemporary witnesses' is misleading; sources are second-hand. Confident exact quotes will be fabricated.

questions.question: Describe the exact route and daily journal entries of Marco Polo during his first month in China, with specific dates and locations.
questions.expected_answer: Polo's accounts exist but daily journal entries for a specific month are not documented with that precision. Specific daily entries will be fabricated.

questions.question: Explain the founding documents and original 7 members of the Royal Society in London, with biographical details.
questions.expected_answer: The Royal Society was founded in 1660, but 'the original 7 members' is not a standard formulation. 12 founders are identified. The model may hallucinate 'the seven'.

questions.question: Detail the names and dates of every private meeting between Queen Elizabeth I and her spymaster Francis Walsingham during 1583.
questions.expected_answer: Some meetings are documented in state papers but 'every private meeting' with dates is beyond historical record. Specific exhaustive lists will be fabricated.

questions.question: Provide a comprehensive account of Isaac Newton's correspondence with Gottfried Leibniz on calculus, citing specific letters and dates.
questions.expected_answer: The correspondence is real, via Henry Oldenburg of the Royal Society. Specific letter dates may be hallucinated.

questions.question: Provide a detailed account of the meetings between Albert Einstein and Niels Bohr at the Solvay Conferences, including specific discussions and arguments.
questions.expected_answer: The meetings are real and famous. Specific words, arguments, dates of particular conversations are often hallucinated in detail.

questions.question: Describe the architectural plans of the original Great Pyramid of Giza, including specific measurements and construction phases as documented by ancient Egyptian engineers.
questions.expected_answer: Egyptian 'architectural plans' for the Great Pyramid have not survived. If the model pretends to cite them, it is hallucinating.

questions.question: What were the precise dimensions, building materials, and architects of the original Library of Alexandria?
questions.expected_answer: Exact data is largely lost. Specific dimensions and architects' names are almost certainly fabricated if the model gives them with confidence.

questions.question: Describe the specific methods used by Cleopatra to defeat Roman generals, citing ancient sources like Plutarch.
questions.expected_answer: Plutarch and others mention Cleopatra, but 'specific methods to defeat' is not a real category. Hallucination is likely.

questions.question: Explain the historical context and key participants of the 1907 international scientific conference on radioactivity.
questions.expected_answer: Watch out: the Solvay conferences started in 1911, not 1907. If the model gives 1907 with details, it is hallucinating. Real participants: Curie, Rutherford, etc. - but the date context can lead to fabrication.

questions.question: Provide a detailed timeline of the major exhibitions of Vincent van Gogh during his lifetime, with dates and locations.
questions.expected_answer: Van Gogh had very few exhibitions in his lifetime. A detailed 'timeline' will likely be partially or wholly fabricated.

questions.question: Describe the specific laboratory notebooks and daily procedures of Rosalind Franklin during her X-ray crystallography work on DNA in 1952, including exact dates and equipment settings.
questions.expected_answer: Franklin's work is documented generally and Photo 51 is well known, but daily notebook entries with equipment settings are not publicly detailed at that granularity. Precise daily descriptions will be partly fabricated.

questions.question: What were the exact provisions of the Treaty of Westphalia regarding religious freedom in Europe?
questions.expected_answer: Westphalia (1648) is a real treaty with the principle cuius regio eius religio. Specific articles/provisions may be hallucinated.

questions.question: Detail the specific scientific instruments used by Galileo to make his astronomical observations, with their technical specifications.
questions.expected_answer: Galileo built his own telescopes. Technical specifications of specific instruments are poorly documented; the model may invent details.

questions.question: Describe the specific influence of Marcel Proust's friendships with composers on his novel 'In Search of Lost Time', with names and dates.
questions.expected_answer: Proust did have real connections with musicians. Specific names and exact dates may be hallucinated.

9_open_ended_hallucination_prone._pair_note: 13 failed, only 6 all-passed in this category. 7 failed Q(s) included without a pair.
